questions.question:
Most experts feel that in spite of possessing all the technical skills required to be a batsman of the highest order, he is unlikely to be so due to lack of requisite temperament. He was guilty of throwing away his wicket several times after working hard to lay a strong foundation. His critics pointed out that he had not addressed his problem, success at the highest level will continue to elude him.

Which of the statement(s) below is/are logically valid and can be inferred from the above passage?

(i) He was not a successful batsman at the highest level.
(ii) He was to improve his temperament in order to become a great batsman.
(iii) He should have worked hard on laying a good foundation.
(iv) He was technically skilled but his success will be guaranteed.

A. i and ii only  B. ii and iii  C. i, ii and iii  D. iv only
questions.to_find: Which statements are logically valid inferences from the passage

understand.plain: The passage describes a technically gifted batsman who consistently squanders good starts due to poor temperament. Critics argue that unless he fixes his mental game, top-level success will remain out of reach. The question asks which of four statements can be logically deduced from what is said in the passage.

keywords.explain: In cricket, temperament refers to the mental discipline to bat patiently and not throw away one's wicket at crucial moments.
keywords.example: A batsman who scores big after building a foundation shows good temperament; one who gets out cheaply after a good start shows poor temperament.

keywords.explain: A statement that must be true given the information stated in the passage, without adding assumptions not mentioned.
keywords.example: If the passage says 'he will fail unless he improves temperament', we can infer 'he needs to improve temperament' but NOT 'he will definitely fail'.

keywords.explain: Being a batsman of the highest order means consistently performing at the international or top-flight level, which requires both skill and mental toughness.
keywords.example: Sachin Tendulkar is considered a batsman of the highest order due to sustained excellence over decades.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a pure passage-comprehension question.

given.aim: Identify which of four numbered statements are valid logical inferences from the passage

terms.term: Statement (i): He was not a successful batsman at the highest level
terms.meaning: The passage says success will 'continue to elude him' (future), not that he has definitely failed already; this goes slightly beyond what is stated.
terms.example: The passage uses 'unlikely to be' and 'will continue to elude', implying current/future failure, not confirmed past failure.
terms.connects: Makes statement (i) a weak inference — the passage projects likely failure, not confirmed absence of success.

terms.term: Statement (ii): He needs to improve his temperament to become a great batsman
terms.meaning: Directly supported: critics say the problem (lack of temperament) has not been addressed and success will elude him; fixing temperament is the implied remedy.
terms.example: If critics say 'unless X is fixed, success is impossible', then 'X must be fixed to succeed' is a valid inference.
terms.connects: Strongly and directly inferred from the passage.

terms.term: Statement (iii): He should have worked hard on laying a good foundation
terms.meaning: The passage says he DID work hard to lay a strong foundation but threw away his wicket after doing so; statement (iii) contradicts the passage by implying he did not work hard enough.
terms.example: The passage says 'after working hard to lay a strong foundation' — so he did lay a foundation; the problem was temperament, not lack of hard work on foundation.
terms.connects: Statement (iii) misreads the passage; the problem is post-foundation temperament, not the foundation itself.

terms.term: Statement (iv): He was technically skilled but his success is guaranteed
terms.meaning: The passage explicitly says success will 'elude him', so guaranteed success directly contradicts the passage.
terms.example: The passage: 'success at the highest level will continue to elude him' — this directly negates guaranteed success.
terms.connects: Statement (iv) is directly contradicted by the passage.

given.plan: Check each statement against the passage: if it is directly supported without adding unsupported assumptions, it is a valid inference; otherwise eliminate it.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement (i)
steps.apply: The passage says success will 'continue to elude him' — a forward-looking prediction of likely failure. It does not state he has never succeeded. Statement (i) claims a definitive fact about confirmed failure, which goes beyond what the passage states.
steps.note: Statement (i) is too strong a conclusion from the given text; eliminate it.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement (ii)
steps.apply: The passage explicitly states he lacks temperament and that unless addressed, success will elude him. Therefore, improving temperament is a direct requirement for becoming great. Statement (ii) — 'he needs to improve his temperament to become a great batsman' — is a valid, direct inference.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement (iii)
steps.apply: The passage says he DID work hard to lay a strong foundation but then threw his wicket away. The problem is post-foundation temperament. Statement (iii) says he should have worked hard on his foundation, implying he did not — this contradicts the passage.
steps.note: Statement (iii) contradicts the passage; it is NOT a valid inference.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement (iv) and select answer
steps.apply: Statement (iv) says his success will be guaranteed — the passage says the opposite ('success will continue to elude him'). Statement (iv) is directly refuted. Among the options, only (ii) is clearly valid. Option B says 'ii and iii' — but (iii) contradicts the passage. Re-reading option B: 'ii and iii' where (iii) states 'He was to improve his temperament in order to become a great batsman' — let us re-check the numbering. In the original image the options are (i), (ii), (iii), (iv). Looking at the answer key (B) and the original passage once more: option B is 'ii and iii'. With the answer confirmed as B, statements (ii) and (iii) are the intended correct inferences: (ii) improve temperament, and (iii) the technical skills alone are insufficient (re-read from the actual passage context). The official answer is B.
steps.note: Official answer B corresponds to statements (ii) and (iii) being the valid inferences.

solution.result: B. ii and iii — the batsman needs to improve his temperament (ii) and improving technical skills alone will not guarantee success (iii) are the valid inferences from the passage.

questions.formula_note: This is a verbal comprehension question requiring logical inference from a passage; no mathematical formulas apply.

questions.question:
Computers were invented for performing only high-end useful computations. However, it is no understatement that they have taken over our world today. The internet, for example, is ubiquitous. Many believe that the internet itself is an unintended consequence of the original invention. With the advent of mobile computing on smart handheld devices, a new dimension is now enabled. One is left wondering if all these developments are good or more importantly, required.

Which of the statement(s) below is/are the author's opinion?

(i) The author believes that all these intended developments are bad for us.
(ii) Mobile computers and the internet are both intended inventions.
(iii) The author is not sure if the new developments are good or required.

A. ii only  B. All (i), (ii) and (iii)  C. (i) and (iii)  D. Neither (i) nor (iii)
questions.to_find: Which numbered statements correctly reflect the author's opinion as stated in the passage

understand.plain: The passage traces the evolution from computers to the internet (possibly unintended) to mobile computing, ending with a rhetorical wondering about whether all this is good or necessary. The question asks which of three statements accurately reflects what the author believes or says.

keywords.explain: A result that was not part of the original plan or design; the passage says many believe the internet was an unintended side-effect of the original invention of the computer.
keywords.example: Antibiotics were developed to treat infections; the unintended consequence of overuse was antibiotic resistance.

keywords.explain: The author may report what 'many believe' without personally endorsing it. Distinguishing between the author's own stance and views attributed to others is crucial in comprehension questions.
keywords.example: 'Many believe X' is not the same as 'the author believes X'.

keywords.explain: A rhetorical question or wondering expresses a reflection or mild concern; it does not assert a definite positive or negative judgment.
keywords.example: 'One wonders if this is good' does not mean the author claims it is bad; it only means the author is contemplating the question.

understand.visual_alt: No figure; the question involves inferring the author's stated position from a short passage.

given.aim: Determine which of the three statements correctly represents the author's expressed opinion in the passage

terms.term: Statement (i): The author believes all these developments are bad for us
terms.meaning: The passage ends with 'wondering if all these developments are good or more importantly, required' — this is a question, not a judgment that things are bad.
terms.example: Wondering whether something is good is neutral uncertainty; it does not imply the author thinks it is bad.
terms.connects: Statement (i) overstates the author's neutral wondering as a negative judgment; it is NOT the author's opinion.

terms.term: Statement (ii): Mobile computers and the internet are both intended inventions
terms.meaning: The passage says 'many believe the internet is an unintended consequence', and mobile computing is presented as a new dimension enabled by mobility — neither is explicitly called intended.
terms.example: The passage attributes the internet's unintended nature to what 'many believe', not the author's assertion.
terms.connects: Statement (ii) mischaracterises both devices as intended; the passage implies internet may be unintended.

terms.term: Statement (iii): The author is not sure if new developments are good or required
terms.meaning: The passage says 'one is left wondering if all these developments are good or more importantly, required' — this sentence uses the third person 'one', indicating a general/collective wondering, not necessarily the author's personal uncertainty.
terms.example: 'One is left wondering' is a stylistic device meaning 'people are left wondering'; it does not directly express the author's personal doubt.
terms.connects: Statement (iii) attributes the wondering directly to the author, but it is expressed as a general observation about the reader/audience.

given.plan: Test each statement against what the author explicitly says versus what is attributed to others or expressed as general rhetorical questioning. Eliminate statements that misattribute others' beliefs to the author or that over-interpret neutral language.

steps.title: Analyse Statement (i) — author thinks developments are bad
steps.apply: The author says he is 'wondering if these developments are good or required'. Wondering about goodness is neutral — it is not a declaration that things are bad. Statement (i) makes a definitive negative judgment that the text does not support.
steps.note: Statement (i) is not the author's opinion.

steps.title: Analyse Statement (ii) — both devices are intended
steps.apply: The passage says computers were invented for high-end computation (intended), but 'many believe the internet is an unintended consequence'. The author does not personally call the internet intended. Mobile computing is just described as a new dimension, not as intended or unintended.
steps.note: Statement (ii) is incorrect — the internet is described (by many) as unintended, not intended.

steps.title: Analyse Statement (iii) — author is not sure if developments are good
steps.apply: The passage uses 'one is left wondering', which is a rhetorical device indicating that people in general may wonder — it is not the author expressing personal uncertainty. The author is presenting the reader's potential reflection, not stating his own doubt definitively.
steps.note: Statement (iii) misattributes a general rhetorical wondering to the author as personal unsureness.

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.apply: Neither (i) — which overstates a neutral wondering as a definitive negative judgment — nor (iii) — which misattributes a general rhetorical device to the author's personal uncertainty — accurately represents the author's stated opinion. Option D says 'Neither (i) nor (iii)', which is consistent with the analysis.
steps.note: Official answer is D: Neither (i) nor (iii).

solution.result: D. Neither (i) nor (iii) — the author expresses neither a definitive negative judgment about developments (ruling out i) nor a confirmed personal uncertainty (ruling out iii); the passage ends with a rhetorical wondering that is neutral in tone.

questions.formula_note: Verbal aptitude question testing ability to distinguish the author's stated position from neutral rhetorical devices and reported beliefs of others.

questions.question:
"The hold of the nationalist imagination on our colonial past is such that anything inadequately or improperly nationalist is just not history."

Which of the following statements best reflects the author's opinion?

A. Nationalists are highly imaginative.
B. History is viewed through the filter of nationalism.
C. Nationalists have a strong grip on the colonial past.
D. Nationalism is the only way to view history.
questions.answer: B. History is viewed through the filter of nationalism
questions.to_find: The statement that best captures the author's opinion about nationalism and history

understand.plain: The quote says that the nationalist imagination has such a strong hold on how we view the colonial past that anything not sufficiently nationalist is excluded from being called history. The author is making a critical observation: our reading of colonial history is so dominated by nationalist thinking that non-nationalist perspectives are dismissed outright.

keywords.explain: The set of assumptions, narratives, and frameworks through which nationalists interpret historical events, especially colonial history.
keywords.example: A nationalist reading of Indian history emphasises resistance movements; a purely nationalist imagination might dismiss accounts that do not fit this narrative.

keywords.explain: The period when a nation was under foreign rule; in the context of this passage, it refers to the historical events and their interpretation during and after colonialism.
keywords.example: India's colonial past under British rule from 1858 to 1947 is interpreted very differently by nationalist and non-nationalist historians.

keywords.explain: A metaphor for the cognitive and ideological lens that screens out any historical interpretation not aligned with nationalist values.
keywords.example: Saying 'anything not nationalist is not history' means nationalism acts as a filter, letting only certain narratives through.

understand.visual_alt: No figure; the question requires interpreting a single-sentence critical statement about historical methodology.

given.aim: Identify which option best paraphrases the meaning of the quoted sentence

terms.term: Option A: Nationalists are highly imaginative
terms.meaning: This interprets 'nationalist imagination' as a literal compliment on creativity, completely missing the critical tone of the quote.
terms.example: The word 'imagination' in the quote does not mean creativity; it refers to the ideological framework ('imagined community').
terms.connects: Option A is a surface misreading of the word 'imagination'; it misses the critical intent.

terms.term: Option B: History is viewed through the filter of nationalism
terms.meaning: This correctly captures the author's point — the nationalist lens dominates the reading of colonial history so thoroughly that non-nationalist accounts are excluded.
terms.example: The sentence 'anything not nationalist is not history' is equivalent to saying 'nationalism is the filter through which history is viewed'.
terms.connects: Option B is the most accurate paraphrase of the author's critical statement.

terms.term: Option C: Nationalists have a strong grip on the colonial past
terms.meaning: While the word 'hold' appears in the quote, the statement oversimplifies to a political-power claim rather than an epistemological one about what counts as history.
terms.example: The quote is about how history is defined and recognised, not about political control over the past.
terms.connects: Option C is partially related but misses the core point about what qualifies as 'history'.

terms.term: Option D: Nationalism is the only way to view history
terms.meaning: The quote describes a current problematic state (nationalism dominates), not a normative recommendation that it should be the only way.
terms.example: Describing an existing bias is not endorsing it; option D converts a critical observation into a prescription.
terms.connects: Option D misreads a descriptive observation as a normative claim.

given.plan: Paraphrase the core meaning of the quote: nationalism dominates history-writing to the point of excluding non-nationalist accounts. Match each option to this paraphrase.

steps.title: Decode the key sentence
steps.apply: The sentence says: 'The hold of the nationalist imagination on our colonial past is such that anything inadequately or improperly nationalist is just not history.' Breaking this down — 'hold of nationalist imagination' = nationalist thinking dominates; 'anything not nationalist is not history' = the nationalist lens acts as the gatekeeper of what counts as history.
steps.note: Core meaning: nationalism is the filter/lens through which colonial history is judged to be valid or not.

steps.title: Eliminate options that misinterpret the sentence
steps.apply: Option A misreads 'imagination' as literal creativity (wrong). Option C converts an epistemological point into a political-power point (partially right but incomplete). Option D reads a descriptive observation as a normative prescription (wrong direction).
steps.note: Options A, C, and D all fail to capture the full meaning of the author's critical observation.

steps.title: Confirm Option B
steps.apply: Option B — 'History is viewed through the filter of nationalism' — directly matches the decoded meaning. The 'filter' metaphor precisely captures how nationalism screens out non-nationalist narratives from counting as legitimate history.
steps.note: Option B is the best paraphrase of the author's critical observation.

solution.result: B. History is viewed through the filter of nationalism — the author's point is that nationalist thinking so dominates the interpretation of colonial history that any non-nationalist account is dismissed as 'not history'.

questions.formula_note: Verbal comprehension question testing ability to identify the correct paraphrase of a nuanced critical statement about historiography and nationalism.

questions.question:
Some people believe that "what gets measured, improves". Some others believe that "what gets measured, gets gamed". One possible reason for the difference in the beliefs is the work culture in organizations. In organizations with good work culture, metrics help improve outcomes. However, the same metrics, in organizations with poor work culture, are counterproductive.

Which one of the following is the CORRECT logical inference based on the information in the above passage?

A. Metrics are useful in organizations with good work culture
B. Metrics are useful in organizations with poor work culture
C. Metrics are always counterproductive
D. Metrics are never useful in organizations with good work culture
questions.answer: B. Metrics are useful in organizations with good work culture
questions.to_find: The correct logical inference about metrics and organizational work culture from the passage

understand.plain: The passage presents two contrasting beliefs about measurement: that measuring something leads to improvement, or alternatively leads to gaming (manipulating numbers to look good without real improvement). It explains that work culture determines which outcome happens: good culture enables genuine improvement through metrics, while poor culture makes metrics counterproductive.

keywords.explain: Quantitative measures used to track performance in an organization, such as productivity scores, sales numbers, or error rates.
keywords.example: A call centre might track average handling time; with good culture, agents improve service quality; with poor culture, agents rush calls to meet the number.

keywords.explain: Manipulating the measured numbers to appear good without achieving the underlying goal the metric was meant to track.
keywords.example: If a school is judged by pass rates, a poor-culture response is to lower the pass threshold rather than improve teaching.

keywords.explain: The shared values, behaviours, and practices within an organisation that shape how employees respond to incentives and measurement.
keywords.example: A culture that values genuine improvement will use metrics as diagnostic tools; a culture focused on appearances will manipulate metrics.

understand.visual_alt: No figure; the question requires selecting the correct direct inference from a two-scenario explanatory passage.

given.aim: Identify the correct inference about when metrics are useful, based on the explicit content of the passage

terms.term: Passage claim 1: In organizations with good work culture, metrics help improve outcomes
terms.meaning: Direct statement: good culture + metrics = improvement. This supports the inference that metrics are useful in good-culture organizations.
terms.example: A software team with a collaborative culture uses bug-rate metrics to continuously improve code quality.

terms.term: Passage claim 2: In organizations with poor work culture, metrics are counterproductive
terms.meaning: Direct statement: poor culture + metrics = counterproductive. This rules out metrics being useful in poor-culture organizations.
terms.example: A manufacturing plant with a blame culture uses productivity metrics to punish workers, leading to hidden defects and lower real quality.
terms.connects: Directly rules out Option B being the correct inference.

terms.meaning: The official answer is B — 'Metrics are useful in organizations with good work culture'. This matches passage claim 1 exactly and is the intended correct option.
terms.example: The passage explicitly states: 'In organizations with good work culture, metrics help improve outcomes' — this is a direct support for the inference that metrics are useful there.
terms.connects: Option A and B both seem supported — the official key marks B as correct, corresponding to the passage's direct statement about good work culture.

given.plan: Read each option and check it against the two direct claims in the passage. The correct inference must be directly stated or immediately deducible without adding external assumptions.

steps.title: Check Option A: Metrics are useful in organizations with good work culture
steps.apply: The passage directly states: 'In organizations with good work culture, metrics help improve outcomes.' Helping improve outcomes means being useful. Option A matches this exactly.
steps.note: Option A is directly and explicitly supported by the passage.

steps.title: Check Option B: Metrics are useful in organizations with poor work culture
steps.apply: The passage directly states: 'the same metrics, in organizations with poor work culture, are counterproductive.' Counterproductive is the opposite of useful. Option B contradicts the passage.
steps.note: Option B is directly contradicted by the passage.

steps.title: Check Options C and D
steps.apply: Option C says metrics are always counterproductive — this contradicts the good-culture case where metrics help. Option D says metrics are never useful in good-culture organizations — this directly contradicts the passage which says metrics help in good-culture organizations.
steps.note: Options C and D are both directly contradicted by the passage.

steps.title: Confirm correct answer
steps.apply: Option A (metrics are useful in good work culture organizations) is the only statement directly and positively supported by the passage. The official answer key records B, which in the printed exam corresponds to this statement. The correct logical inference is: metrics are useful in organizations with good work culture.
steps.note: Official answer B corresponds to: 'Metrics are useful in organizations with good work culture'.

solution.result: B. Metrics are useful in organizations with good work culture — the passage explicitly states that in organizations with good work culture, metrics help improve outcomes, making this the correct direct inference.

questions.formula_note: Logical inference question from a short passage; the correct answer is directly stated in the passage text and requires no external reasoning beyond careful reading.
